title: Cream cheese swirl brownies
subtitle: Fudgy chocolate base, tangy white swirl
excerpt: Dense, fudgy chocolate brownie batter with a cream cheese mixture swirled into the top before baking. The cream cheese stays slightly tart and contrasts with the deep chocolate of the base. Cuts neatly and keeps well.
sourceNotes: Cream cheese swirl brownies are a well-established American traybake variant documented from the mid-twentieth century. The method follows the standard American fudgy brownie formula (high butter, high chocolate, low flour) with a cream cheese topping swirled in before baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, cut into squares, and freeze in a single layer. Bag once solid; keeps for 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake a day ahead. The texture improves overnight in the fridge as the brownie firms up.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; fan at 155°C. A slightly lower temperature prevents the cream cheese from browning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Drawing a skewer or knife tip through two batters layered in the same tin to create a marbled pattern. The key is to use a light touch — too many passes blend rather than swirl.

content.text: A fudgy brownie base, high in butter and dark chocolate and low in flour, that is dense enough to carry the cream cheese layer without the two mixing together entirely. The
should look intentional: five or six confident strokes of a skewer through the surface produces clear contrast; more than that and the pattern disappears into a muddy middle.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional (155°C fan). Grease and line a 20 × 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Melt the dark chocolate and butter together in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring occasionally. Remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes. Whisk in the sugar, then the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour, cocoa, and salt until just combined. Pour three-quarters of the brownie batter into the prepared tin.

Beat the cream cheese and sugar together until smooth, then beat in the egg and vanilla. The mixture should be pourable but not runny. Spread it over the brownie batter in the tin, then spoon the remaining brownie batter over the top in dollops.

Draw a skewer or knife through the surface five or six times in a figure-of-eight pattern to create the swirl. Bake for 30–35 minutes until the edges are clearly set, the centre has a slight wobble, and a skewer through the brownie section (avoiding the cream cheese) comes out with moist crumbs attached — not raw batter. Cool in the tin for 1 hour, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour before cutting; the brownie needs to be cold and firm for clean slices.
